solution:
If linear momentum are equal then wavelength also equal.

The momentum p and energy E of a photon is given by,

p=hλ, E=hcλ; where c is speed of light and h is Plank's constant.

On decreasing wavelength, momentum and energy of photon increases.

subject: Physics
solution:
The dimensional formula for Planck's constant $h$ is obtained from $E = h\nu$.

$[h] = \dfrac{[E]}{[\nu]} = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-2}}{T^{-1}} = ML^2T^{-1}$

The dimensional formula for stopping potential $V_0$ is obtained from $eV_0 = E$.

$[V_0] = \dfrac{[E]}{[e]} = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-2}}{AT} = ML^2T^{-3}A^{-1}$

Work function is a form of energy, so its dimensional formula is the same as that of energy.

$[\text{Work function}] = ML^2T^{-2}$

Threshold frequency has the dimensional formula of frequency.

$[\text{Threshold frequency}] = T^{-1}$

Therefore, the correct matching is A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II

subject: Physics
solution:

So, for the first source,

n1×hcλ1=200   ...1

And, for the second source,

n2×hcλ2=200   ...2

From equations (1) and (2), it implies that

n1n2=λ1λ2=300500⇒n1n2=35

subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the proton is given by

λp=h2mpqpV   ...1

The formula to calculate the de Broglie wavelength of the alpha particle is given by

λα=h2mαqαV'   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the required ratio.

λpλα=mαmp×qαV'qpV   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

λpλα=41×2×41×2=4:1

$\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi} \\
& \mathrm{mv}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}} \\
& \lambda=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{rh}}{\mathrm{nh}} \\

& \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_1 \mathrm{n}_4}{\mathrm{n}_1 \mathrm{r}_4}=\frac{5.3 \times 10^{-11} \times 4}{1 \times 84.8 \times 10^{-11}} \\
& \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}=\frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
Physics
solution:
The force experienced by the electron in the electric field is given by $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$.

Since the charge of an electron is $-e$ and the electric field is $\vec{E} = -2E_0\hat{i}$, the force is:

$\vec{F} = (-e)(-2E_0\hat{i}) = 2eE_0\hat{i}$

The acceleration of the electron is:

$\vec{a} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{m} = \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}\hat{i}$

Using the first equation of motion, the velocity of the electron at time $t$ is:

$\vec{v}(t) = \vec{V} + \vec{a}t = \left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)\hat{i}$

The de Broglie wavelength of the electron at time $t$ is:

$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{m|\vec{v}(t)|} = \dfrac{h}{m\left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)}$

We are given that $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{4mv_0}$, which implies $h = 4mv_0\lambda_0$. Substituting this into the expression for $\lambda$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{4mv_0\lambda_0}{m\left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)}$

Dividing the numerator and the denominator by $mv_0$, we get:

$\lambda = \dfrac{4\lambda_0}{1 + \dfrac{2E_0 e}{m}\dfrac{t}{v_0}}$

Answer: $\dfrac{4\lambda_0}{\left[1 + \dfrac{2E_0 e}{m}\dfrac{t}{v_0}\right]}$

solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $h u = W + KE$.
With zero kinetic energy, $h u = W$.
The frequency is $ u = rac{W}{h} = rac{110 imes 10^{-20}}{6.63 imes 10^{-34}} = 16.59 imes 10^{14}$ Hz.
The angular frequency is $\omega = 2\pi u = 2\pi imes 16.59 imes 10^{14}$
$= 104.25 imes 10^{14} = 1.04 imes 10^{16}$ rad/s.

subject: Physics
solution:
The data given is 

ϕAl=4.1 eV

ϕAu=5.1 eV

The energy is given by 

hν=ϕ+KE⇒hν=ϕ+eV0

where V0 is the stopping potential and K=eV0

Thus, the above equation can be written as

eV0=hν-ϕ

⇒V0=hνe-ϕe

The equation is of the form of y=mx+c. The slope is 

m=he.

Since the Planck constant h and charge of an electron is a constant e, the slope does not depend on the work function. Hence the ratio of the slope of the plot of stopping potential and frequency for both the given metals is 1.

subject: Physics
options: All the metals will give out photo electrons with same kinetic energies.
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons is given by:

$K_{ ext{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of incident radiation and $\Phi$ is the work function of the metal.

The work function is related to the threshold frequency $ u_0$ by $\Phi = h u_0$. From the given graph, the x-intercept represents the threshold frequency. We can observe that:

$ u_0(X_1) < u_0(X_2) < u_0(X_3)$

Therefore, the work functions of the metals follow the order:

$\Phi(X_1) < \Phi(X_2) < \Phi(X_3)$

For the same wavelength $\lambda$ of incident radiation, the term $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ is constant. Thus, the kinetic energy $K_{ ext{max}}$ will be greatest for the metal with the smallest work function.

Since metal $X_1$ has the minimum work function, it will emit electrons with the greatest kinetic energy.

Answer: $X_1$
